EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This river flooding episode continues from May. A series of weather disturbances brought heavy rainfall to the Pacific Northwest. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff to produce flooding on the Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt runoff to produce flooding on the Columbia River. The Columbia River at Vancouver (VAPW1) crested at 17.43 feet at 07:30 am on June 2nd, 2011. The Clark County Parks Department sustained significant damage at Captain William Clark Park in Camas and inumdation/minor damage at three other parks (Vancouver Lake, Frenchman's Bar, and Salmon Creek Trail) along the Columbia, totaling about $50k - $100k. The Port of Camas-Washougal reports about $100k estimated cost to repair two sections of levee that had significant erosion damage. Vancouver Public Works reported damage to the Vancouver Renaissance Walkway, with a rough estimate of repair costs $100k - $1M.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow pounded parts of southeast Washington as a moist frontal boundary stalled over the region, from the Northeast Blue Mountains into the Washington Palouse. This is the same frontal boundary that sagged southward the previous day on February 28th with heavy snowfall totals over the in the Northeast Blue Mountains eastern portions of the Washington Palouse. This band slowly migrated northward through the morning hours with heavy snowfall ending by the mid to late morning hours. Numerous locations received around a foot of total snowfall from February 28th into March 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A substantial rain event in mid January resulted in a strong, thick rain crust on an already established snow pack in the east slopes of the Cascades.  Mid-January to mid-February was mild with only modest amounts of snowfall added to the slopes.  Temperatures were much colder through the end of February and a series of storms from February 28th through March 4th resulted in heavy snowfall over the thick icy crust.  This series of events resulted in an unstable slab of snow susceptible to avalanche formation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 28 year old male skier triggered an avalanche at the top of Hooky Bowl located near Mt. Cashmere.  The slab depth, at the point where the avalanche was triggered, was just over a half a foot in depth and increased in depth up to three and one quarter foot.  The horizontal dimension of the slab was approximately 3 feet by 200 feet.  The skier was carried through several groups of trees and funneled down a steep gully.  After the avalanche ended, the skier was found alive with one leg dismembered and the other leg suffering significant fractures.  Other skiers arrived within two minutes of the event and performed lifesaving measures immediately, however, patient eventually died as a result of his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts accompanied a cold front passage on the evening of March 13.  The strong winds were reportedly responsible for blowing over large trees three miles south of Pullman.  Wind gusts were estimated to be as strong as 60 mph, however this may be an overestimate as the strongest gust recorded in the area was 45 mph by the Pullman ASOS around the same time the report came in.
Snow fell over much of western Washington.  Amounts varied from 1-10 inches with one site in Mount Vernon reporting 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 4 inches of snow fell over most of the foothills, however some locations got between 6 and 14 inches.  Driving in some locations became extremely difficult.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary trough of low pressure off the Pacific Northwest Coast steered a deep fetch of subtropical moisture over the Cascade Mountains from March 29th through the 31st. This resulted in several rounds of moderate to heavy rainfall mainly near the immediate crest.  Mild temperatures and breezy winds accompanying the subtropical moisture feed resulted in excessive mid-elevation snowmelt. Consequently, saturated soils became unstable in some locations resulting in small debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported minor flooding of First Creek which resulted in some damage along First Creek Road.

Three-day rainfall amounts near the debris slides ranged from two to five inches.  Highway 2 was closed from March 31st through April 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This heavy snowfall episode continues from February. A strong front moved into the region Sunday evening and remained fairly stationary over the area through Monday. The frontal system produced very heavy snowfall in the Cascades with some snow accumulations at elevations as low as 300 to 600 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This heavy snowfall episode continues from February. Heavy snow was measured in Stevenson, Washington by two different NWS employees with 5 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool upper-level trough engulfing much of the western CONUS coupled with sufficient surface heating the afternoon of May 29th supplied enough instability to generate isolated thunderstorms across the Washington Palouse.  One of the stronger cells that tracked between Pullman and Colton produced a large rope-like funnel cloud which several trained spotters observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained spotters viewed a large rope-like funnel cloud attached to a storm that moved from approximately five miles north of Colton toward the town of Pullman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring season continued for the Inland Northwest as another wet storm system moved through the region May 21-22nd producing in the neighborhood of half to one and a half inches of liquid precipitation over Northern Washington. This resulted in additional rises of many creeks and streams which were already swollen from wet spring conditions and excessive snowmelt throughout the month.  Streams and creeks continued to run out of their banks and flood roadways throughout the East Slopes of the Northern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On May 22nd, Salmon Creek Road was closed between Spring Coulee Road and Danker Cut-Off Road due to water and debris flowing over the roadway from Salmon Creek. A combination of high spring runoff and log jams in the creek was responsible for the flooding. The road was later reopened but closed on and off for the next few weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring season continued for the Inland Northwest as another wet storm system moved through the region May 21-22nd producing in the neighborhood of half to one and a half inches of liquid precipitation over Northern Washington. EVENT_NARRATIVE: On May 22nd, Sinlahekin Road was closed between Fish Lake and Blue Lake due to water and debris flowing over the roadway. The road was later reopened but closed on and off for the next few weeks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Pacific Northwest. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the low triggered widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding. A 75 yard wide mudslide closed Highway 410 at milepost 87. As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Touchet Snotel (7).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Microburst like wind gust blew trampoline over and kicked up several pieces of debris.  Several tree limbs 1 to 3 inches in diameter have fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds picked up across the western Columbia Basin behind a fast moving cold front.  Winds were particularly strong during the evening hours on Thursday.  A surface low moved into southern British Columbia during the afternoon hours resulting in a tightening pressure gradient across eastern Washington.  This was combined with strong upper level winds mixing down to the surface on the lee side of the Cascades resulting in a short period of strong wind gusts during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residence of Crescent Bar, approximately 2.5 miles west-southwest of Quincy, reported strong winds around 1945PST Thursday evening.  Multiple large, shallow rooted trees were toppled over and numerous heavy red clay tiles were blown off on the south side of the Crescent Bar Condos.  Two windows were shattered by the roof tiles as they were blown off.  Funneling effects down into the canyon off the east slopes of the northern Cascades or developing mountain waves may have been a contributing factor to the localized nature of the wind damage from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring season continued for the Inland Northwest during the last week of May as another, wet storm system moved through the region producing in the neighborhood of half to one and a half inches of liquid precipitation over Northern Washington May 25-26th. Many basins were already swollen from wet conditions and excessive snowmelt earlier in the month and soils were very saturated and unstable in some locations. As a result, rivers, small streams, and creeks continued to rise near bankfull to above flood stages.  Additionally, several landslides occurred in the Okanogan National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads sustained water damage from flooding creeks and streams within the Okanogan National Forest.  These roads include West Chewuch Road, Volstead Road, Cub Creek Road, Boulder Creek, and Harts Pass.  The extent of the damage is not known at this time and damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved over the Pacific Northwest. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the low triggered widespread thunderstorms with heavy rainfall and isolated large hail. This combined with the abundant spring snow-pack and wet ground to cause flooding. A 75 yard wide mudslide closed Highway 410 at milepost 87. As the upper low brought colder air. late season heavy snow ended the episode in the Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts in inches included Touchet Snotel (7).

A wet spring season resulted in saturated soils across the Inland Northwest.  Rivers, small streams, and creeks began to rise as warming temperatures on May 13th and 14th melted mountain snowpack across the east slopes of the northern Cascades and Northeast Mountains.  Warm and dry weather transitioned to cooler but considerably wetter conditions over the weekend May 14th and 15th.  An upper level low pumped a considerable amount of moisture into the region with an extended period of heavy rainfall over the Cascades to the Okanogan Highlands early Saturday morning through Sunday morning.  This heavy rainfall slowly pushed to the northeast into the Spokane Area and Northeast Mountains by Sunday afternoon into Monday.  Forty-eight hour rainfall totals ranged from one to three inches across the east slopes of the northern Cascades, one to one and a half inches across the Spokane Area, to one to two and a quarter inches across the Northeast mountains.  In addition to the flooding, landslides were reported, and even a few trees were downed as winds picked up across the Okanogan Highlands Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snow melt resulted in flooding along Colockum Road.  Kingsbury Bridge was undercut by Colockum Creek resulting in its closer.  Further down the creek flooding was reported on Tarpiscan Road, which blocked vehicle access to the Washington State University agricultural research station.  Colockum Creek flowed in the basement of a rental home, forcing the renters to evacuate.  Property damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet storm system moved through the region producing in the neighborhood of half to one and a half inches of liquid precipitation over Northern Washington May 25-26th.  Soils were already very saturated and unstable from an ongoing wet spring and the addition of rainfall on the 25th resulted in a large landslide just outside Conconully, Washington located in the East Slopes of the Northern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major landslide occurred in the USFS land above Conconully.  Three parties of campers were trapped at the Salmon Meadows Campground after mud and debris flowed across Salmon Creek Road.  One camper was almost swept off a USFS road by the slide but was able to navigate his truck and 35 foot camper in reverse quick enough to reach safe ground.  The slide was caused when a tributary above the road dammed during this spring's high runoff and broke loose Wednesday evening.  The slide was estimated to be 70-foot wide and anywhere from five to ten feet deep.  There were no injuries.  County officials were able to clear a path through the mud and debris on Thursday allowing the campers to evacuate.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of saturated soils and strong winds resulted in two trees toppling over onto highway 14 east of Republic.  The first tree fell just after 2350PST on May 13th fourteen miles east of town and the second fell around 0430PST nine miles east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system off the Northwest Coast resulted in a strengthening southwest to northeast surface pressure gradient across northeastern Washington during the early morning hours.  Subsequently, winds began to pick up out of the northeast in the neighborhood of up to 15-20 mph sustained and gusting up to around 30 mph.  Although these winds were not abnormally strong, their northeasterly direction were favorable for developing relatively high swells of up to one and a half feet along the western shore of Deer Lake in southeastern Stevens County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Waves produce by these winds were large enough to dislodge one launch dock and damage two boat docks, with one boat dock able to hold 20 boats and the other 24 boats. A couple of boats parked at the docks were damaged as well.  This damage was reported to have occurred sometime between the nighttime hours on May 13th through the early morning hours on May 14th.  Mesonet sites in the area reported winds picking up just before 0800PST on May 14th.  Winds from these Mesonet sites ranged from 8 to 18 mph sustained with gusts between 18 and 30 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low situated itself over western Oregon.  A moist disturbance rotating around the low into southeastern Washington resulted in widespread precipitation across the area.  Cooling temperatures during the early morning hours dropped snow levels down to around 3,500 feet with heavy snow reported through the morning.  This band of precipitation moved slowly to the north and east through the day with snowfall reported above 5,000 feet as it moved into the Okanogan Highlands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall was mainly confined to those elevations above 5,000 feet.  Moses Mountain SNOTEL recorded about four inches of total snowfall based off snow depth measurements.  Sherman Pass webcam showed the roadway to be snow covered with low visibilities down to one mile or less at times during the early afternoon hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several landslides occurred in the Okanogan National Forest within the War, Scaffold, and Eagle creeks area of the Twisp River. The largest slide was along the War Creek Ridge which inundated about 100 yards of the Forest Service Road leading to the War Creek Trailhead. A survey done from a helicopter shortly after the slide indicated two long channels, which was estimated to be some 30 feet deep. The time of the slide is estimated based on reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring season continued for the Inland Northwest during the last week of May as another, wet storm system moved through the region producing in the neighborhood of half to one and a half inches of liquid precipitation over Northern Washington May 25-26th. Many basins were already swollen from wet conditions and excessive snowmelt earlier in the month and soils were very saturated and unstable in some locations. As a result, rivers, small streams, and creeks continued to rise near bankfull to above flood stages.  Additionally, several landslides occurred in the Okanogan National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A massive landslide occurred in the Pearrygin Lake area just outside Winthrop, WA|on the afternoon of May 31st.  The Okanogan Sheriff's Office reported that the landslide was about 40 acres long and covered 200 yards of Walter Road and majority of a property located at the end of Walter's Road.  The mudslide damaged and spun the homeowner's travel trailer around, damaged a hot tub, pushed a pickup truck 75 feet, but missed his home by 50 feet.  The property owner stated that he had roughly 13 thousand in property damage; 40-60 thousand in cleanup cost; and additional loss due to the range and alfalfa fields being covered in mud and large logs.  The property owner estimated it would be three years before the fields could be planted again.  Agriculture losses were estimated to be near 17 thousand dollars over the three year period.  The slide may have also compromised the aquafer in the area.  Springs which fed the aquifer were torn apart and are currently running down the top of the slide much like a stream.  Residents in the area are digging new holding ponds to mitigate the loss of spring water.  Additionally, a U.S. Forest Service vehicle and four employees were trapped behind the slide but were able to walk through the slide that afternoon and return to town.  The slide was believed to be caused when Pearrygin Creek got dammed upstream and changed course toward Walter Road.   There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dust devil was thought to be the cause of minor damage at a resident's house just southwest of Clayton, Washington.  The region was located under a cool upper-level trough with fair cumulus at the time of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident located southwest of Clayton, WA sustained minor damage to her trailer when a strong dust devil moved across her property.  The dust devil ripped siding off of both sides of her trailer.  The siding landed in her neighbor���s horse pasture. It also picked up her trampoline and threw it several feet.  There were no injuries.  Damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of warm temperatures and series of wet storm systems brought rises to many rivers, creeks, and streams across portions of Northeast Washington.  Many of the already swollen basins overflowed their banks and flooded roadways, driveways, and fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The north end of Deep Lake overflowed its banks flooding portions of Deep Lake Boundary Road with six inches of water.  Since May 26th, small creeks flowing into the lake have spilled out of their banks and flooded several fields and driveways leading to homes in the area.  No property damage has been reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably cool storm system and ample moisture resulted in moderate to heavy precipitation across Northeastern Washington on the 26th of May. Precipitation began as rain however as a cold front tracked through the region, snow levels lowered near 4000 feet resulting in late-season heavy snow across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Liquid precipitation amounts from SNOTEL sites within the Northeast Mountains received anywhere from 0.60 to 1.50 inches of liquid. Those sites with snow pillows recorded 4 to 10 inches of snowfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Liquid precipitation amounts from SNOTEL sites within the Okanogan Highlands received anywhere from 0.60 to 1.20 inches of liquid. Those sites with snow pillows recorded 4 to 8 inches of snowfall.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES CENTRAL CASCADES AND PASSES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell over much of western Washington.  Amounts varied from 1-10 inches with one site in Mount Vernon reporting 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWAC station at Paradise received 19 inches snow in 24 hours. NWAC station at Alpental received 12 inches snow in 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front brought strong southerly winds to the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment with sustained wind of 43 kts and gusts to 59 kts. Strong wind was also reported at Toke Point with sustained wind of 35 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front brought strong southerly winds to the south Washington Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment with sustained winds of 35 kt and gusts to 45 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front brought strong southerly winds to the south Washington Coast and southwest Washington interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment and Clatsop Spit with sustained winds of 41 kt and gusts to 50 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific cold front brought strong southerly winds to the south Washington Coast and southwest Washington interior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Naselle Ridge with gusts to 66 kt. Strong winds were also reported at the Tidewater RAWS with gusts to 54 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the region began to break down as a cold storm system dropped in out of the Gulf of Alaska on the night of the 26th.  Moist westerly flow enveloped the region as this upper level low sagged down the British Columbia coastline.  Low level flow was primarily out of the east and southeast through the day on the 27th, which created favorable orographic lift in combination with mid level warming.  Accumulating snowfall continued through the early afternoon hours on the 28th, especially closer to the crest.  Storm total accumulations ranged from half a foot furthest from the crest to a foot and a half closer to the crest.

A strong Pacific cold front brought strong southerly winds to the Pacific Northwest this afternoon and evening...especially along the south Washington coast and interior portions of southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting between 45 kts and 55 kts were reported in adjacent zones to the north and south. It is reasonable to assume that this zone had similar winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A modified arctic air mass situated over the region was overrun by a subtropical moisture fetch resulting in heavy snow over portions of central and northeast Washington.  Most locations received three to six inches of new snow with locally higher amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread three to six inches of snow fell overnight with an isolated report of nine inches.

Precipitation ended with a brief period of freezing rain producing a light glaze of ice on top of the snow.  A newspaper article reported several accidents along interstate 90 during the morning commute in the Spokane area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air trapped in the lee of the Cascades was overrun by subtropical moisture resulting in light freezing rain through the early morning hours of the 13th.  Although ice accumulations were light, roads were extremely icy during the morning commute resulting in numerous accidents and school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely icy roads helped to cause multiple vehicle collisions and closure of several Grant County schools includng Ephrata, Moses Lake, Royal City, and Warden.  Around forty vehicle collisions were reported to the sheriff's office.  Many of those collisions caused only minor damage to vehicles and no serious injuries.  There was one serious injury which resulted when debris from the collision of a truck and parked vehicles (from an ealier accident) struck a pedestrian.  A propane semi-truck rolled over and shut down Dodson Road for nearly ten hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to the Lower Columbia Basin of Washington and heavy snow in the mountain areas.  The heavy snowfall closed Interstate 90 over Snoqualmie Pass for several hours.  School was closed in Cle Elum Roslyn.  Peak winds reported in miles per hour included  8 miles northwest of West Richland (58), 15 miles west of Basin City (67), and 14 miles north northwest of West Richland (59).  Snowfall amounts reported in inches included Cle Elum (17), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (23.5), Easton (15), 4 miles west of Roslyn (20), Snowden (16), 5 miles northeast of White Salmon (21), 8 miles northeast of Lyle (10), Touchet SnoTel (8), 13 miles east northeast of Walla Walla (8), and Goldendale (5).

A strong cold front moved through the region from west to east.  This front produced gusty southwest winds across the Columbia Basin with gusts between 30 and 50 mph common through the afternoon and evening hours.  Breaking mountain waves on the lee side of the Northern Cascades resulted in localized high wind gusts of 50 to 72 mph in southern Grant County.  Numerous power outages and broken tree branches were reported in this area along with localized areas of blowing dust, which included the towns of Mattawa, Royal City, and Othello.  Several shingles were blown off a residential home and two inch diameter tree limbs reportedly broken off near Beverly.

An upper-level trough slowly sagged into the Pacific Northwest on the 28th of February.  Strong west to southwest flow along and ahead of the leading cold front resulted in a twelve hour period of heavy snow across the Washington Palouse and Northern Blue Mountains.  Snow began to fall on the 28th of February and continued through the afternoon hours of March 1st.  Elevation played a large role in snowfall totals which ranged from nine to fifteen inches above 2000 feet to less than one inch at elevations below.  Although never reported, blowing snow may have had a significant impact on travel across the Northern Blue Mountains.  Alder Ridge RAWS at an elevation of 4500 feet reported sustained winds between 15 and 25 mph with gusts to 40 mph.

A cold easterly wind through the Columbia River Gorge was keeping cold air trapped in the Gorge as a strong Pacific frontal system moved inland. This system spread precipitation over the Gorge starting as snow and changing over to freezing rain as the air mass warmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Hood River reported 4.5 inches of new snow accumulation, with similar snowfall on the Washington side of the Columbia River north of Hood River.  Washington State Route 14 was impacted with snow and ice accumulation between Washougal and the White Salmon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Pacific Northwest bringing strong southerly winds to the south Washington Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported at Cape Disappointment with sustained winds of 45 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold upper-level trough settled into the Pacific Northwest on the 21st and 22nd of February.  A combination of upsloping flow and several smaller disturbances embedded within the trough utilized the deep instability in place resulting in several rounds of heavy snow, especially across the Northeast and Blue Mountains.  Snowfall amounts over the three day period in these mountain zones ranged from one to three feet.  Several rounds of heavy snow also impacted the upper Columbia Basin and Spokane area mainly on the 23rd and 24th but due to the highly convective nature of the snowfall, storm total snowfall amounts varied from four to fourteen inches.  On the evening of the 23rd, a strong arctic front moved into the region.  A combination of heavy snow bands developing along the front and strong northeast winds behind the front resulted in blizzard conditions for several hours across Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from four to eight inches through midnight on the 24th of February.

An unseasonably warm and wet period resulted in minor flooding along the Palouse River in Whitman County. The Inland Northwest remained in the warm sector of a flat ridge with warm air advection into the region. The warm air scoured out the cold air in place, resulting in sharp rises to snow levels across extreme eastern Washington. Record November snowfall, followed by above average December snowfall, set the stage for a healthy low to mid elevation snow pack for many areas across extreme eastern Washington. In these areas, snow levels rose sharply during this period as a series of warm and wet weather systems produced heavy rainfall at times at mid and low elevations. Heavy rain, combined with the melting snow pack, resulted in small streams, as well as, main stem rivers running above flood stage in portions of extreme eastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Palouse River at Palouse overflowed its banks inundating portions of downtown.  The hardest hit were low lying areas, where water surrounded homes and covered nearly all of Hayton Green Park on the west end of town.  A basement of a local resident and a former school gymnasium were flooded on Main Street.  Additional flooding was reported along the Palouse River upstream in Latah County.  The Palouse River at Potlach went above its flood stage of 15 feet at 1630PST on the 16th; crested at 16.5 feet at 530PST on the 17th; and then went back below flood stage at 615PST on the 18th.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures at around 1500 feet above ground level rose above freezing as a warm front began to move across the region.  Surface temperatures remained below zero through much of the morning as the warm air advection accompanying the front took some time to scour out the colder air already in place.  Light precipitation began to fall due to weak isentropic accent.  The pocket of warm air above the surface was enough for much of the Upper Columbia Basin and the Spokane Area to receive freezing rain.  Precipitation amounts were relatively light for the morning hours in the range of 0.01 to 0.05 inches for these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 150 traffic accidents were attributed to light ice accumulation on area roadways due to freezing rain.  Numerous damaged or disabled vehicles were reported on I-90 through Spokane and Spokane Valley with traffic brought to a standstill in spots along the freeway.  At least six accidents were reported in Lincoln County.  One collision at the intersection of State Route 2 near Oehlwein Road east of Davenport resulted in 3 injuries of two teenage girls and one adult male.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sagged southeastward across the region and stalled over southeast Washington.  Temperatures quickly dropped to around freezing Tuesday morning as colder air moved in behind the front.  Heavy snowfall was observed for a 24 hour period between Tuesday morning and Wednesday morning.  A weather spotter near Anatone reported a storm total snowfall accumulation of nine inches.

Rainfall on January 15th may have been a contributing factor for loosening rocks and other debris from a hill side along highway 17 at Soap Lake.  A car traveling along the highway crashed into the rocks and rolled.  The driver was injured in the accident, but the passenger was uninjured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exact time of the landslide is unknown; however, the accident was reported to have occurred Sunday night.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind was measured on Denny Peak, elevation 5528 feet, by a NW Weather and Avalanche Center (NWAC) weather station.  Gusts over 75 mph occurred off and on through the period.

Strong downsloping winds down the east slopes of the Cascades resulted in windy conditions just east of the slopes.  A strong upper level jet coupled with winds of 60 plus knots about 2,000 feet above the ground resulted in strong wind gusts Sunday night as these stronger winds above the surface mixed down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds reported in Desert Aire near Mattawa resulted in two buildings receiving roof damage.  This was an isolated report of wind damage as the strongest wind gusts seemed to stay primarily to the southwestern portion of Grant County.  Land-line telephone service was out for a portion of Grant County as well.

Cool, wet spring conditions across Eastern Washington during early June continued to saturate already unstable, liquefied soils over the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades.  This resulted in another large landslide in the Lake Chelan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide occurred on the morning of June 10th and covered South Shore Drive between Lake Chelan Yacht Club and Cove Marina.  The slide covered the equivalent of one city block.  It took four workers roughly three days to clear the road.  The exact time of the slide was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low situated across southern Alberta, Canada resulted in numerous convective showers across the northeast mountains of Washington.  A few of these showers contained embedded thunderstorms with periods of heavy rain mixed with pea to penny sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain that resulted in water gushing down a sloped gravel driveway.  Minor sections of the driveway were damaged.  Pea sized hail was also reported with the thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall accumulation of 1.10 inches was reported within a span of 45 minutes to one hour.

This river flooding episode continues into June. A series of weather disturbances brought heavy rainfall to the Pacific Northwest. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff led to flooding on the Columbia River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt runoff to produce flooding on the Columbia River. The Columbia River at Vancouver (VAPW1) crested at 17.43 feet on June 2nd, 2011.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of warm temperatures and series of wet storm systems brought rises to many rivers, creeks, and streams across portions of Northeast Washington.  Many of the already swollen basins overflowed their banks and flooded roadways, driveways, and fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chewelah Creek flowed out of its banks on May 26th flooding portions of Highway 395, Lincoln Street, and Chewelah City Park.  Water had dropped significantly by the |evening of May 28th with only some residual standing water on Lincoln Street.  Damage estimates were only for the park and playground equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tornado with a peak wind speed estimated near 75 mph. Light to moderate damage to metal roofs on out buildings. Light damage to two houses - missing shingles and siding damage. Large branches snapped off several trees. Tornado estimated on the ground for less than 2 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado with a peak wind speed estimated near 75 mph. Light to moderate damage to metal roofs on out buildings. Light damage to two houses - missing shingles and siding damage. Large branches snapped off several trees. Tornado estimated on the ground for less than 2 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of mid and high elevation rainfall impacted Central Washington March 25th through March 27th.  Two-day rainfall amounts ranged from one to one and a half inches.  The combination of the rainfall and a slow snowmelt resulted in increasingly saturated soils which eventually became unstable.  A large slide which occurred southwest of Peshastin was believed to have been caused by the excessive rainfall causing an underground stream to surge out of the steep hillside.  This stream had surfaced several hours prior to the actual slide according to reports from residents near the 6100 block of Campell Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large debris slide destroyed outbuildings, vehicles, and portions of an orchard in the 6100 block of Campbell Road in Peshastin, WA on the afternoon of March 27th.  Damage from the mudlside mainly impacted one resident who reported that his barn, shop, pump house, and three large vehicles were completely destroyed.  The resident at this property reported that the slide ripped a hole through his land that he described as a 600-foot long channel 100 feet wide and 10 to 50 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: End of March heavy rainfall on melting snowpack along the east slopes of the Washington Cascades caused flooding along numerous streams in western Kittitas and Yakima counties. Reported rainfall included 2.5 inches in Easton and .69 inches in Cle Elum. Water was over the roads in 12 locations in upper Kittitas County and resulted in washouts of shoulders and some mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding continued from March 31 with the gage at Easton cresting at 1 foot above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong front moved into the region Sunday evening and remained fairly stationary over the area through Monday. The frontal system produced very heavy snowfall in the Cascades with some snow accumulations at elevations as low as 300 to 600 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This heavy snowfall episode extends into March. Heavy snow was measured in Stevenson, Washington by two different NWS employees with 5 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pepper Creek SNOTEL site measured 26 inches of new snow. Heavy snow was also measured at the Calamity SNOTEL site with 20 inches of new snow and at Brush Prairie with 11 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were reported by an NWS employee in Hockinson, Washington with sustained winds of 39 kts and gusts to 50 kts. Strong winds were also reported by another NWS employee in Vancouver, Washington with large broken tree branches and minor damage to home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain and snow melt in the east slopes of the northern Cascades resulted in flooding and debris flows across the area.  Three-day rainfall totals ranged from two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A blocked railroad culvert resulted in water flooding out onto Chumstick Highway near Freund Canyon about 1.3 miles north of Leavenworth.  The water was about a foot deep across the road for a brief period resulting in its closure.  Parts of the road were damaged by the flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain and snow melt in the east slopes of the northern Cascades resulted in flooding and debris flows across the area.  Three-day rainfall totals ranged from two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hillside gave way in the Icicle Canyon resulting in a landslide the hit at Eight Mile Campground.  The slide started as high as 1,000 feet above the valley floor and stretched across Icicle Creek Road at the bottom of the hill.  The slide cut off access to four homes and Eight Mile Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rainfall along a cold front overnight on March 15th gave way to clearing skies during the early morning hours on March 16th.  Radiational cooling dropped temperatures to below freezing in some spots in the Spokane Area.  Icy conditions followed as some of this water from earlier rainfall remained on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pickup truck heading west on Government Way lost control near the intersection of Aubrey L. White Parkway and flipped off the road.  The 47 year old male driver was pronounced dead on the scene shortly after law enforcement arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: End of March heavy rainfall on melting snowpack along the east slopes of the Washington Cascades caused flooding along numerous streams in western Kittitas and Yakima counties. Reported rainfall included 2.5 inches in Easton and .69 inches in Cle Elum. Water was over the roads in 12 locations in upper Kittitas County and resulted in washouts of shoulders and some mudslides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuing from March 31, the river gage at Horlick crested at .5 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary area of low pressure centered over northeastern Washington the evening of April 25th into the morning of April 26th produced a narrow swath of heavy precipitation over northern Spokane and southern Pend Oreille Counties.  Some mountain locations received upwards of one to three inches of liquid which primarily fell as snow.  The hardest hit area was Mount Spokane where two to three feet of heavy wet snow fell.  The weight of the snow coupled with already saturated soils resulted in 40 to 50 uprooted or snapped mature trees along highway 206 from Bear Creek Lodge to Mount Spokane Ski Lodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At elevations generally above 2500 feet, approximately forty mature trees fell down along Highway 206 from northeastern Mead to Bear Creek Lodge with an additional 15 to 20 trees uprooted from Bear Creek Lodge to the Mount Spokane Ski Lodge.  The Quartz Peak SNOTEL recorded 28 inches of snow; Ragged Mountain SNOTEL 22 inches; and the park ranger from Mount Spokane State Park reported 30 to 36 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds produced from thunderstorms in northern Spokane were thought to be responsible for blowing a trampoline into a nearby power station around 4:00 PM on the evening of April 30th resulting in power outages for nearly 11,000 residents.  The storms were not severe and nearby weather stations recorded wind gusts to just 30 mph, however this is not to say localized higher gusts did not occur at the location of the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trampoline was blown into a power substation resulting in power outages to approximately 11,000 customers.  Avista Utilities estimated damage cost to the power station to range from 10 to 15 thousand dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain and snow melt in the east slopes of the northern Cascades resulted in flooding and debris flows across the area.  Three-day rainfall totals ranged from two to five inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landslide moved down Tumwater Canyon and covered highway 2 about a mile west of Leavenworth.  The slide closed Highway 2 in both directions.  The road was covered with six to eight feet of mud and debris for approximately one-eighth of a mile.  The highway remained closed until Saturday afternoon 300PST April 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific weather system became established in mid January that produced copious amounts of rainfall with unseasonably warm conditions in parts of Washington. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt caused rapid runoff with 4 rivers and 7 different warning points reaching flood stage.   Flood warnings were first issued on the morning of January 15 and the last flood warning ended on January 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the high water on the Yakima River, numerous small streams and creeks in the west part of the county flooded with roads covered with water in housing developments along Interstate 90 west of Cle Elum and in the northern and western sections of Ellensburg.  Forty homes, 64 roads and 3 commercial properties were impacted.  Most of the home impacted were surrounded by water with residents cut off from the property by water covered roads.  Damage to roads and public works in Kittitas County is estimated at 1.5 million dollars with residential and business damage near 650,000 dollars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Yakima County, the flooding was contained to the low areas along the Yakima River including the Selah Golf Course and the Yakima Greenway.   The Yakima River at Parker crested at 12.2 feet just after midnight on January 18th, 2.2 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system combined with strong winds aloft produced high wind gusts. Klickitat PUD reported 23 power poles down.  Two trees fell in the Goldendale area. On the east side of Goldendale, a strong wind gust totaled an empty semi-truck as it traveled south bound on Highway 97.  Peak winds recorded in miles per hour were 15 miles west of Benton City (83), 8 miles northwest of West Richland (96), 22 miles southeast of Sunnyside (70), 3 miles west of Sunnyside (59), 21 miles south of Vantage (77), 19 miles northwest of West Richland (62), 13 miles west northwest of Bickleton (78), and 10 miles east northeast of Goldendale (62).

Amounts reported in inches included 3 miles northwest of Cle Elum (6), 7 miles north northwest of Easton (9), 2 miles east of Cle Elum (6), 8 miles southeast of Cliffdell (7), 2 miles northwest of Ellensburg (5), and 1 mile north of Thorp (5).
A human caused wildfire, which started near Highway 97, burned 3600 acres 12 miles northeast of Goldendale.  The fire also destroyed 100 structures including 29 residences.

Cold air dammed into the East Slopes of the Washington Cascades was overrun by moisture with subtropical origins.  This resulted in several hours of heavy snow across the Wenatchee Valley with snow amounts ranging from one to three inches near Wenatchee to as much as eight inches in Chelan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was reported in Chelan and five inches in Ardenvoir.  Most accumulations were at or above elevations of 1300 feet with a mix of sleet, snow, and freezing rain observed in the lowest elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold front brought high winds. Peak wind gusts in miles per hour included Hanford Emergency Ops Center (72), Gable Mountain (71), Yakima Barricade (77), Hanford (61), Rattlesnake Springs (69), Hanford 200 West (71), Hanford Army Loop (69), Vernita Bridge (78), Richland Airport (58). Tree branches up to 1.5 inches in diameter were downed by the winds 8 miles west northwest of Connell.  A wildfire in White Swan, fanned by the high winds up to 69 mph, was carried from a house to a logging mill and into the town. The wildfire burned 20 homes.  A trailer was blown over west of Ellensburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain created icy roadways in Grant County resulting in more than 25 collisions during the Friday morning commute.  Three county roads were temporarily closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dynamic and potent frontal system moved across north central Washington during the morning hours.  Thunderstorms developed just out ahead and along the cold front with periods of moderate to heavy rain reported in the Wenatchee Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall resulted in cherry damage to several orchards from the Wenatchee area, northward toward Pateros.  It was reported that up to approximately fifteen percent or more of the cherry orchards were damaged.  The Skeena variety of cherries was most damaged as the rainfall resulted in severe cracking of fruit.  The ASOS at the Wenatchee Airport reported 0.30 inches of rainfall between 0600PST and 1200PST.  Damage estimations were based on a loss of 150 tons of cherries combined with the additional costs of renting roughly a dozen helicopters used to dry the orchards.  One helicopter pilot died when the low-flying aircraft clipped a power-line and crashed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tight south-north pressure gradient at the surface combined with strong winds aloft produced high wind gusts across western and northern Benton County.  Peak wind gusts reported in mph included 8 miles northwest of Richland (58), 11 miles west of Basin City (59), Hanford EOC (60), Rattlesnake Springs (64), and Edna (66).

A Pacific storm system tracking from the Gulf of Alaska into southwestern Canada ushered a strong cold front across Eastern Washington on the afternoon of November 11th.  This was a classic gradient driven wind pattern for Eastern Washington, which consisted of a surface low tracking into southern Alberta while a strong high pressure cell shifted inland off the eastern Pacific Ocean.  Wind gusts of 40 to 55 miles per hour were observed during the late afternoon over the Spokane Area.  The strong winds toppled several trees and caused numerous power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranging from 45 to 55 mph were common in the Spokane area between 1530 and 1730 PST.  The Spokane International Airport ASOS measured a peak gust of 52 mph.  Several trees were downed mainly on the western slopes of the South Hill.  Some trees toppled on power-lines causing an estimated 1200 power outages.  A 60 foot Ponderosa Pine tree top fell on a South Hill home causing significant damage to a third of the home.  The exact location was unknown.  Local media reported downed trees at the intersections of 43rd and Scott, Hatch and Tomaker, and Hatch and Westchester Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of orographics and isentropic ascent centered in the warm sector of an approaching Pacific storm system resulted in heavy snowfall across the northeast mountains of Washington.  Warmer air eventually moved into the region and snow levels climbed above valley floors with heavy snow continuing in the mountains through midday on the 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Valley snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 with locally up to 6 inches near the Canadian Border.  49 degrees North Ski Resort reported 23 inches of snow over the two-day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper-level trough sagged into the Pacific Northwest during the afternoon of October 5th. Stable rainfall was common through most of the morning and early afternoon over eastern Washington.  During the early afternoon, a jet streak rounding the base of the trough coupled with cooling aloft, and brief thinning of the overcast skies on the eastern flank of the precipitation shield contributed to a rapid, very short-lived window of surface based instability and low-level wind shear over the Washington Palouse. This resulted in the development of two intense updrafts and formation of a pair of funnel clouds, one which briefly touched down over open wheat fields in northern Whitman County. There was no damage or injuries related to the tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farmer in northern Whitman County spotted two funnel clouds the afternoon of October 5th approximately thirteen miles northwest of Saint John.  One funnel cloud briefly touched down over open wheat fields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across eastern Washington from the southwest through the afternoon and evening hours on November 16th.  This front acted to moisten the atmosphere ahead of a cold front that moved into northeast Washington during the overnight hours on the sixteenth.  Heaviest snowfall occurred out ahead and along the cold front with up to four to five inches of new snowfall within a five hour span that stretched into the early morning hours of November 17th.

High winds caused multiple power failures for 1800 customers in the Tieton area.  The first outage was around 11 am due to downed power lines and the second was around 4 pm.  The wind also fanned a spontaneous combustion wildfire at the Boise Cascade mill site in Yakima.

A deep closed low pressure system moved into southwestern Washington.  Bands of snow developed along a TROWAL (TROugh of Warm Air ALoft) that extended from northern Idaho into eastern Washington.  This feature was nearly stationary overnight on November 17th through November 18th.  Snow showers persisted into the early hours of November 19th before this feature weakened and shifted eastward.  Heavier snowfall reports ranged from six inches to a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds brought high wind to the north coast and to the area around Lake Lawrence in the southwest interior.  The central coast had about 9000 lose power, and the Bellingham area had scattered power outages and a car damaged by tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Lake Lawrence reported a 61 mph gust.  This spot is known for having high wind when other places have much less wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low situated across southern Alberta, Canada resulted in numerous convective showers across the northeast mountains of Washington.  A few of these showers contained embedded thunderstorms with periods of heavy rain mixed with pea to penny sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: It was estimated that 0.75 inches to 1.00 inches of rain fell within ten minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins. This caused moderate flooding along the Pend Oreille River and several of its tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooded river. The most severe flooding along the Pend Oreille River occurred between Albeni Falls and Box Canyon Dams.  Flooding through the first week of July began on June 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bead Lake flooded its banks and water inundating a few basements of lakeside homes. The exact number of homes impacted was unknown at the time and damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of warm temperatures earlier in the month led to rapid rises on small streams as snow melt rapidly from mid and high elevations.  High temperatures at low to mid elevations were in the upper 70s to low 80s June 4th through June 6th.  Although temperatures cooled into the low 70s through June 10th, small streams continued to rise as snow melt from high elevations made its way into low elevation drainages.  A wet storm system on June 8th exacerbated the situation with flows on small creeks and streams peaking by June 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide was reported over upper Icicle Road about ten miles west of Leavenworth.  The slide was about 200 yards wide and 10 to 12 feet deep.  About 30 people were stranded at a campground above the slide.  Some damage was reported to the road.  Damage reported was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeastern Washington was under an unstable and active weather pattern as a cold upper level trough approached from the west and warm, moist air streamed across the area from the southwest. Solar heating through the late afternoon hours was enough for air parcels to break through the capping inversion with scattered convection firing up through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved into Asotin County from northeast Oregon during the late afternoon or early evening hours.  The public reported hail stones up to the size of golf balls as the storm moved over Montgomery Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins. This caused moderate flooding along the Pend Oreille River and several of its tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooded river. The most severe flooding along the Pend Oreille River occurred between Albeni Falls and Box Canyon Dams.  Flooding through the first week of July began on June 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Diamond Lake flooded its banks and water inundating a few basements of lakeside homes and private roads. The exact number of homes impacted was unknown at the time and damage costs were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins. This caused moderate flooding along the Pend Oreille River and several of its tributaries. Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooded river. The most severe flooding along the Pend Oreille River occurred between Albeni Falls and Box Canyon Dams.  Flooding through the first week of July began on June 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread flooding was observed along the Pend Oreille River through the first week of July 2011 impacting the communities of Newport, Dalkena, Usk, Cusick, Ruby, Blueslide, Lost Creek, Ione, and Metaline Falls. Some of the worst flooding was noted along the following roads: Alaska Way, Davis Road, McCloud Creek, Deeter Road, Riverbend Loop, Sandy Shores, Yergens, Le Clerc Road, Pow Wow Park Road, Dike Road, Needles Road, and Main Street in Metaline Falls; four private roads including Beehive Lane, Bear Paw Drive, Echo Lane, and Hawk Lane were also flooded. Numerous homes along these roads suffered flooding with as much as two to three feet of water in their basements and significant damage to docks, sheds, and other outbuildings. The town of Metaline was forced to bypass sewage treatment and allow its storm drains to flow directly into Pend Oreille River. The flooding had enormous impacts on recreation with many boaters avoiding the river due to a no-wake restriction in place. Additionally, the Kalispels' 37th Powwow and 4th of July celebration were cancelled due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post frontal showers developed in the wake of a cold front late in the afternoon across southern Pend Oreille County.  A shallow layer of instability off the surface and ample low level moisture resulted in quick developing convective cells that produced heavy rainfall and pea sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed out a section of a dirt road approximately three-quarters of a mile north of Newport on the west side of the Pend Oreille River.  Recent road work by the planned utility development installing a fiber-optic cable along this section of the roadway likely made the hillside more susceptible to the washout.  Property damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man was suspected of drowning in the ocean off the South Washington coast near the community of Seaview, Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group gathering on the beach approximately one mile south of Seaview,  Washington contacted Pacific County to report that a man had been in the water and was no longer in sight at 10:15 pm, Sunday. Pacific County launched two boats and the Coast Guard launched a boat and a helicopter to search for the man. The body was found the recovered the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Salmon Fire, located five miles northwest of Omak, was a human caused fire that burned 1910 acres.  The fire was not completely suppressed until September 6th 2011.

The Highway 17 Fire, located four miles northwest of Coulee City, was a human caused fire that burned 600 acres. The fire threatened 6 homes and burned mostly sage and grass.  Bonneville Power Administration reported minor char to some transmission poles.

The Tumwater Canyon Fire, located six miles northwest of Leavenworth burned 458 acres of timber, grass, and shrubs in steep terrain of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. The cause of the fire is still under investigation. Highway 2, between Coles Corner and Icicle Creek was closed from August 17th until the evening of August 19th. Tumwater Campground was temporary closed due to the wildfire.

Post frontal showers developed in the wake of a cold front late in the afternoon across southern Pend Oreille County.  A shallow layer of instability off the surface and ample low level moisture resulted in quick developing convective cells that produced heavy rainfall and pea sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell at rate of approximately one and one quarter of an inch per hour resulted in fast flowing water down a sloped front yard of a residence.  Loose soil was easily carried by the runoff, which created a flow of mud that covered the driveway of the residence.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire started around noon in Grant County about five miles north of Marlin.  Strong winds through the day carried the fire into Lincoln County before a brief period of rainfall later in the afternoon helped fire fighters put out the blaze.  The fire burned approximately 3,000 acres of sagebrush and destroyed one machine shed filled with farm equipment.
A wildfire burned 408 acres just east of Tonasket, Washington.  The fire which burned through sagebrush and scattered ponderosa pine briefly threatened thirteen homes and burned one outbuilding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marlys Road wildfire initiated near Hanging Rocks off Highway 20, roughly 20 miles east of Tonasket, and burned to Big Boulder Lane.  The fire burned 408 acres along with one outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Washington Flats Fire, located three miles northwest of Grand Coulee, was a human caused fire that burned 1100 acres of heavy sage and grass. The fire threatened some homes resulting in level one evacuations but the fire was contained before any property's were damaged.

The Bell Plain Complex, located four miles northeast of Pomeroy, consisted of seven fires ignited by lightning on the evening of August 29th.  The fires burned 4002 acres of grassland along with some grain fields.  The fires burned close to some homes forcing temporary evacuations.  One spark landed on the roof of one house but it was quickly extinguished before any damage occurred.
The Salmon Fire, located five miles northwest of Omak, was a human caused fire that burned 1910 acres. The fire began on August 29th and was suppressed September 6th.

The Barber Mountain Fire, located thirteen miles northwest of Oroville, WA burned a total of 497 acres in Washington and 40 acres in British Columbia, Canada.  The fire burned through mostly grass and small brush.
A strong south-north pressure gradient produced damaging winds. Power lines, trees, and tree branches were knocked down in Walla Walla and near Dayton.

A cool upper-level trough centered over Eastern Washington produced unstable conditions on the afternoon of June 1st.  Over northern Spokane County, scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon.  One storm which developed over the northwest portion of Spokane quickly intensified and became nearly stationary for roughly 30 to 45 minutes.  This storm dumped an exceptionally high amount of pea-size hail and very heavy rain which quickly overwhelmed the city's sewer infrastructure.  This resulted in water flowing down several streets and flooding of low spots.  Rainfall amounts under this storm were near one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced in the neighborhood of one inch of rain and large amounts of pea size hail over portions of Northwest Spokane between 1900PDT and 2000PDT.  The bulk of the precipitation fell within 30 to 45 minutes causing drainage systems to become overwhelmed.  This resulted in several low crossings of highway 291 between Division Street and Nine Mile Road to quickly flood with anywhere from six inches to a foot and a half of water.  Roughly forty basements in the area also became flooded with several inches of water.  In the Indian Trails area where the terrain is steep, as much as three inches of water was observed flowing down the streets and carved out two to four foot trenches on a steep embankment going through the neighborhood park.  According to city officials, the city's sewer infrastructure did not sustain any damage however several public properties submitted claims due to water damage in their basements.  Twenty-five residents submitted claims with the city with an additional ten to twenty properties assumed to have gone through private insurance companies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a very wet April and May, warmer June temperatures began to melt an above normal mountain snowpack increasing flows into already swollen river basins.|This caused minor flooding along the Okanogan River along with several of its smaller tributaries.  Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of a log jam on the Oak Street bridge and washout of a dike at Pine Street in the city of Okanogan resulted in seepage of the Okanogan River into a few basements of homes along Second Avenue.  Damage costs were estimated.

Agriculture fields, recreation sites, roadways, as well as homes were impacted by the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of landslides and flooding along creeks and rivers resulted in the closure of numerous campsites within Perygian State Park through the first two weeks of June.  Damage costs were estimated based on an average loss of revenue from closed campgrounds.

High temperatures at low to mid elevations were in the upper 70s to low 80s June 4th through June 6th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Silver Falls Creek (near the upper end of the Entiat drainage) went out of its banks and flooded two camp sites at the Silver Falls Campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper level trough resulted in unstable weather conditions across eastern Washington.  Scattered showers with embedded thunderstorms developed in the Okanogan Valley to the northern portion of the Waterville Plateau during the afternoon hours.  A few cells in particular, in the area of Pateros, Brewster, and Bridgeport, produced small hail that resulted in damaged crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size, location, and crop damage were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds to 30 mph caused a wildfire to grow rapidly in southeast Yakima County.  The fire near Konnowac Pass grew to 1200 acres.
A neighbor's grinding tool sent sparks into a yard which started a grass fire which then spread into the house.

Eastern Washington remained under an unstable weather pattern as two upper level storm systems pivoted around a broad long wave trough.  The first wave approached the region on May 27th and exited into Montana on May 30th.  On the heels of this first wave was the second, which moved into the region on May 30th and the 31st.  Okanogan County saw pulse type thunderstorms containing periods of heavy rain and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size, location, and crop damage are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Mount Vernon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted near Mount Vernon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salmon Creek Road was closed June 1st between Spring Coulee and Danker Cutoff roads due to flooding of the Salmon Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather disturbances over the weekend brought sub-tropical moisture to the Pacific Northwest producing heavy rainfall over the region. The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff led to flooding on several rivers and streams in Southwest Washington as well as landslides and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with snowmelt runoff to produce flooding on the Cowlitz River. The Cowlitz River near Kelso crested at 21.5 feet on January 16th at 20:50 PST.
